# Events and Input Differences

Mouse, keyboard, scroll, and clipboard behavior across platforms.

## Mouse Button Numbering

| Button | Windows | Linux | macOS |
|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| Left | Button-1 | Button-1 | Button-1 |
| Middle | Button-2 | Button-2 | Button-3 |
| Right | Button-3 | Button-3 | Button-2 [66] |

### Cross-Platform Right-Click

Use the `<<ContextMenu>>` virtual event instead of binding `<Button-3>` directly [65]:

```python
widget.bind("<<ContextMenu>>", show_context_menu)
```

This handles Button-2 vs Button-3 differences and Control-click on macOS [65].

**Note:** For Windows+Linux only (no macOS), `<Button-3>` is consistent for
right-click on both platforms [66].

## Scroll Wheel Events

| Platform | Event | Delta |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Windows | `<MouseWheel>` | `event.delta` = 120 per notch [67] |
| macOS | `<MouseWheel>` | `event.delta` = 1 per notch [67] |
| Linux/X11 | `<Button-4>` (up) / `<Button-5>` (down) | No delta — use `event.num` [67] |

### Windows Focus Quirk

On Windows, `<MouseWheel>` events go to the window with **keyboard focus**, not the
window under the mouse pointer. Linux sends to the window under the pointer [68].

### Cross-Platform Scroll Pattern

```python
import sys

def on_scroll(event):
    if event.num == 4 or event.delta > 0:
        direction = -1  # Scroll up
    elif event.num == 5 or event.delta < 0:
        direction = 1   # Scroll down
    else:
        return
    widget.yview_scroll(direction, "units")

# Bind both event types
widget.bind("<MouseWheel>", on_scroll)      # Windows/macOS
widget.bind("<Button-4>", on_scroll)        # Linux scroll up
widget.bind("<Button-5>", on_scroll)        # Linux scroll down
```

[67][70]

### Future: Tk 8.7 (TIP 474)

TIP 474 will unify mousewheel handling in Tk 8.7: Button-4/5 events on X11 will be
translated internally to `<MouseWheel>` events [69]. Applications written for Tk 8.6
will need adjustment as delta magnitudes change.

## Keyboard Modifiers

### Meta vs Alt

`Meta` and `Alt` are distinct modifiers in Tk [71]:

- `<Alt>` maps to Alt_L/Alt_R keysyms
- `<Meta>` maps to Meta_L/Meta_R keysyms
- If no Meta keys exist on the keyboard, `<Meta>` bindings never fire [71]

### Platform-Specific Warnings

| Modifier | Warning |
|----------|---------|
| Command/Option | Do not use except on macOS — fires under inappropriate conditions on other platforms [71] |
| Mod4 (Windows key) | Do not use cross-platform — "Windows keys do not function as Modifier keys on Windows" [71] |
| Caps Lock + Shift | Reversed on X11 (Shift undoes Caps Lock); NOT reversed on macOS [71] |

### Safe Cross-Platform Modifiers

Stick to `<Control>`, `<Shift>`, and `<Alt>` for cross-platform bindings [71][72].

## Clipboard

### Linux Dual Clipboard

Linux/X11 has two clipboard mechanisms [73]:

| Selection | Trigger | Paste |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| PRIMARY | Text selected with mouse | Middle-click |
| CLIPBOARD | Ctrl+C | Ctrl+V |

Windows and macOS have only CLIPBOARD [73].

```python
# Read PRIMARY selection (Linux only)
root.selection_get(selection="PRIMARY")

# Read CLIPBOARD (all platforms)
root.clipboard_get()
```

### Clipboard Persistence Bug

Tkinter does not preserve clipboard contents on window closure (CPython #40452) [74].
When the tkinter process exits, clipboard data owned by it is lost.

## Key Events

| Key | Keysym | Notes |
|-----|--------|-------|
| Return (main keyboard) | `<Return>` | Text widget handles this [72] |
| Enter (numpad) | `<KP_Enter>` | Text widget does nothing by default [72] |
| Numpad digits (Num Lock on) | `KP_0` through `KP_9` | Platform-dependent [72] |
| Numpad digits (Num Lock off) | `KP_Up`, `KP_Down`, etc. | Navigation keysyms [72] |

## Gaps and Limitations

- `<<ContextMenu>>` virtual event availability depends on Tk version
- Scroll wheel normalization will change in Tk 8.7 (TIP 474) — code targeting
  both Tk 8.6 and 8.7 will need version checks
- Clipboard persistence on exit has no clean solution in Tk 8.6
